title: Ensure that the default administrative credential file permissions are set to 600

input_row.title: Ensure that the default administrative credential file permissions are set to 600
input_row.description: Ensure that the admin.conf file (and super-admin.conf file, where it exists) have permissions of 600 .
input_row.rationale: As part of initial cluster setup, default kubeconfig files are created to be used by the administrator of the cluster. These files contain private keys and certificates which allow for privileged access to the cluster. You should restrict their file permissions to maintain the integrity and confidentiality of the file(s). The file(s) should be readable and writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the following command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf On Kubernetes version 1.29 and higher run the following command as well :- stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/super-admin.conf Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf On Kubernetes 1.29+ the super-admin.conf file should also be modified, if present. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/super-admin.conf
input_row.default_value: By default, admin.conf and super-admin.conf have permissions of 600 . Internal Only - General

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit mentions specific commands to check file permissions, which implies that the control can be verified through Kubernetes API or kubectl commands.
- The rationale emphasizes the importance of file permissions for security, which aligns with standard Kubernetes security practices.
- The control is about verifying file permissions of kubeconfig files, which is a standard Kubernetes service property.
- The audit does not require subjective review or access to the host OS, as it can be evaluated through the Kubernetes API.

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_kubeconfig_file_permissions_set_to_600

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the command `kubectl exec -it <control-plane-pod> -- stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf` to check the permissions of the `admin.conf` file.
- For Kubernetes version 1.29 and higher, also check the `super-admin.conf` file using `kubectl exec -it <control-plane-pod> -- stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/super-admin.conf`.
- The expected configuration for both files is that their permissions should be `600` or more restrictive.
- Pass criteria: The output of the `stat` command should return `600` for both files, indicating that only the owner has read and write permissions. If the permissions are less restrictive (e.g., `644` or `755`), it would indicate a compliance failure.
